name: The Perfect Tense
summary: The everyday past tense of spoken German, built with haben or sein plus a participle.
explanation:
In spoken German, the perfect tense is how you talk about the past. It has two parts: a helper verb (haben or sein) in second position, and a past participle at the very end.

Most verbs use 'haben': 'Ich habe Fußball gespielt' (I played football). Verbs of movement or change of state use 'sein': 'Ich bin nach Berlin gefahren' (I drove to Berlin), 'Er ist eingeschlafen' (He fell asleep).

Regular participles follow the pattern ge- + stem + -t: spielen -> gespielt, machen -> gemacht. Many common verbs are irregular and end in -en: gehen -> gegangen, sehen -> gesehen, trinken -> getrunken.

conjugation.sein verbs: movement or change of state (gehen, fahren, kommen, werden)

examples.de: Sie ist um acht Uhr aufgestanden.
examples.en: She got up at eight o'clock. (sein)

commonMistakes: Choosing the wrong helper. Movement and change-of-state verbs take sein, not haben.
Leaving the participle in the middle: it must go to the end of the clause.
Forgetting the ge- prefix on most participles: it is 'gemacht', not 'macht'.
Adding ge- to verbs that do not take it, like those ending in -ieren: 'studiert', not 'gestudiert'.
Using -t on irregular participles: it is 'gegangen', not 'gegeht'.
tip: When in doubt about haben or sein, ask: did the subject move from A to B or change state? If yes, use sein. Otherwise use haben.
